Thanks for continuing the work on this @nucleogenesis! I can see that you've dug into a lot of things here, and that you're really working across a lot of the plugins to get things resolved. 

My main issue with this is although it may work more smoothly (I haven't done robust manual QA yet, but I can already tell from the code that there will be improvements), the loading state still seems inconsistent and a bit confusing while reading through. 

For example - in the AppBarPage, you've added `v-if="!$store.state.core.loading"` on the main-wrapper div, which makes a lot of sense to me. But there's still a `loading` prop, and the `KLinearLoader` is visible based on that prop. 

Within `FacilityAppBarPage` you are passing

>  <AppBarPage :title="title" :loading="$store.state.core.loading">

My suspicion is that this combination of "what is loading and where is it coming from" is just an oversight from refactoring, but I think it's worth cleaning up now, across these files and making sure the same type of thing isn't popping up in other places. I can see myself coming to this code later and wondering "why both? maybe the prop isn't even passed in? has it been deleted in the parent and now it's just undefined and not doing anything?"

I can see how tangled up a lot of this is (and you've alluded it it a few times during standups 😄) and I don't want to send you too far down a rabbit hole. I also know your feelings about Vuex! But, I think having a secondary goal of a consistent pattern for using the loading state(s) -- and documenting it, even if only in a few code comments in the most high level shared page components -- would be really helpful to our future selves. I suspect you are already working towards identifying and implementing that consistent pattern, but writing it out might be just the reference point you need to go back through and ensure consistency within and across plugins and pages.  

I'm happy to talk more about this and brainstorm some ideas with you and the pros and cons of approaches. I think it's fine to use both Vuex and props, but in my opinion, it's confusing to use both in the same component. Something like the vuex in the highest parent component, and then passing down the props to other child components as needed could work, and might eliminate the need to be using vuex everywhere. I could imagine there might be cases where that won't work though, or where it might be a little more complex.

Let me know your thoughts on this and if you'd want to talk though anything more. Another option might be to split this into several issues and once we establish a consistent pattern, divide up the work per-plugin so that it doesn't all have to be on you. 

Thanks again for the ongoing efforts here - I know this is not an easy problem to sort out.

Thanks @arun-mahasenan.

Some suggestions/comments below.

It looks like this implementation lets you parametrize _any_ field. As discussed on the design doc, I still suspect that that is not optimal: there are some fields where it does not make semantic sense to parametrize, like `skip_flake8`. 

A major downside of parametrization if you're not careful is dependency inference will cause ambiguity. We have some blessed fields like `resolve` where will update things to handle that, but it seems very wrong if we were to try to write something generically that has `skip_flake8` in the key for module_mapping - you should be able to depend on something regardless of `skip_flake8`.

Further, it's much easier to loosen the restriction than to add it.

I know you're trying to land this before heading out for a couple days, so this doesn't need to block landing this PR. But what do you think about this, and if we could in a followup add `ParametrizableFieldMarker`?

Thought about this some more. Posting some comments I made internally here for the record. 

Making manifest.file a list is probably a nonstarter in my opinion:

- Potential impact on `Manifest.from_file()`, `Manifest.from_topdir()`, `Manifest.from_data()`, `Manifest.__init__()` needs to be investigated
- Impact on west manifest subcommands needs to be investigated (this breaks `west manifest --path` for sure -- others?)
- Getting the syntax right needs to be fixed (comma as separator does not work without other changes as I've commented above)
- Precisely specifying the semantics needs to be done still (what if I "git pull" and one or more of the files goes away? any other edge cases?)
- Extending `west init` to support this needs to be investigated (do we just allow `--mf` to be given multiple times?)


The impact on the API is also pretty unclear to me and it looks like there are dealbreakers:

- For example, `west.manifest.Manifest.abspath` is an attribute that contains the absolute path to the manifest file. With multiple files, this seems broken. Why does this matter? Because you can ask for `west.manifest.Manifest.from_topdir()` to get "the" Manifest object from a workspace topdir, for example.
- Would west need to have to have multiple Manifest objects in flight at any given time in this idea? That would break the `west.commands.WestCommand.manifest` attribute, which both built-in and extension commands rely on to investigate the workspace.

TL;DR west has the assumption that there is one and only one manifest file pretty deeply baked into some core abstractions and I think it is likely that it is too late to change this.

For reviewers, the general code path here is as follows:

1. Users call `my_bulk_writer.create(...)` (or any of the other CRUD methods)
2. The `BulkWriter` instance holds on to the new write operation and finalizes a new `BulkWriteBatch` instance in one of two scenarios:
  a. There are 20 such pooled write operations, or
  b. The new write operation touches a document already involved in a different write operation currently queued
3. Once a `BulkWriteBatch` operation is created, it is added to a queue (`my_bulk_writer._queued_batches`), and the first domino in the sending chain is called, `_send_until_queue_is_empty()`.
4. This method, `_send_until_queue_is_empty()`, calls `_request_send()`, which blocks until all rate-limiting rules agree the batch is able to be sent. Note that we are not yet parallelized, so this properly blocks.
5. The first domino method, `_send_until_queue_is_empty()`, calls the first method that is parallelized, `_send_ batch()`. A few notes about this new method:
  a. `_send_batch()` is optionally (and automatically!) parallelized with a decorator that re-routes execution to `my_bulk_writer._executor`.
  b. `_send_batch()` routes the RPC's response to `my_bulk_writer._process_response()`, which begins invoking registered callbacks for each write in the batch. All of this work still happens in a different thread via the executor, so the main thread is not held up by any of this work.
6. Now back in `_send_until_queue_is_empty()` and thus, on the main thread, the `BulkWriter` instance checks to see if the queue is in fact empty, and, if it is not, calls itself.

These are the core ideas behind the current implementation, but a few more moving parts are added in the next PR which allow for error handling and retries.

This looks like it should work, but I'm worried that it leaves our internal representation of WDL types a bit half-refactored: we represent pair types and map types with Python objects whose structure mirrors the structure of the types, but we don't do the same for optionals and arrays, and all the terminal types are still strings.

I think all WDL types ought to be represented as `WDLType` subclasses, even terminal ones, and `WDLType` shouldn't extend `str`.

(If we were really clever we could make the `WDLType` subclasses metaclasses, so they could be the types of the objects we pass around at runtime, but that might be impossible because, among other problems, calling `WDLArrayType(WDLIntType)` is going to want to produce a new, distinct type every time you write it. So we probably will have to stick with using `list`, `dict`, and a couple special classes like `WDLPair`.)

I also don't understand the `.` vs `_` hack and why it's needed or why it works.

If I grok this correctly, the problem is that `make_file_id` is not aware of the `overwrite_mode` of the workspace and should behave differently. It should not try to avoid clashes because that would lead to inconsistent state, i.e. more files with different IDs instead of overwriting existing files.

One solution could be to add a new kwarg `overwrite` to the `make_file_id` method. Not ideal because that requires all the procesors to be adapted and can lead to easy-to-miss inconsistencies across processors.

Since `overwrite` is a state of the `OcrdWorkspace` class and there is no link back from `OcrdMets` to the workspace it represents, we cannot go `OcrdFile` -> `OcrdMets` -> `OcrdWorkspace` - but maybe this should be possible. We could add a reference from OcrdMets to OcrdWorkspace in the latter's `__init__`.

Then we could check `OcrdFile.mets.workspace.overwrite_mode` and skip the loop to create unique IDs.

Wow, @DaelonSuzuka , that's indeed a lot of work—thanks! Its a sufficiently big extension of QtPy (with many new modules, new errors, and other changes) that I think it makes sense to ship it in 1.2, assuming @dalthviz agrees.

I highly recommend applying my GitHub suggestions first, which are generally straightforward and uncontroversial, before addressing the higher level comments and feedback, assuming you don't disagree with them; otherwise, they are likely to get outdated/lost.

Some high-level feedback and suggestions:

* [ ] Right now, since QtPy is still using the implicit ("flat") package layout rather than the now-recommended explicit `src` layout, this means that `module_report` is a top-level import package equal to `qtpy` itself and will be included in the distribution package and installed in the user's environment as a separate import package, which is of course not what we want. We could exclude it with some futzing in the setuptools configuration, but IMO its still confusing to have two top-level import packages, one of which is just a development-focused helper tool. 

  Therefore, after consultation with you and @dalthviz , I suggest we do one of the following (there is a third option, making it part of the `qtpy` package itself, but I don't think that is more compelling than either of the other two for now):
  
  * Stick `module_report` inside a `tools` directory (which wouldn't be importable and is ignored by default in the new Setuptools 61 auto-discovery) and run it from there when needed *(simplest solution to the immediate problem)*
   * Make `module_report` its own separate repo/distribution package (Better usability for us and others without bundling it in QtPy itself, and if you want to help maintain it, you could much more easily be added as a Collaborator on that repo, but is more initial work, more overhead and less directly coupled to QtPy's own development)

  Making it a separate repo, installable (at least) from GitHub, is the "nicest" solution, but also the most work, up front and (to some extent) over time, so whether this is viable depends on whether you want to help maintain it, and also on if it more generally useful to application developers rather than just development of QtPy itself. So IMO, just keeping it in a `tools` directory is probably the best approach for now unless and until those other conditions are a compelling enough reason to do otherwise (which we could always do in the future). 

* [ ] If we're going to check the output files into the repo, we should run `module_report` in our CIs to catch any changes and either update the files or take other action accordingly. However, given the results can vary between OSes (e.g. `DBus` on Windows) and distributions (`pip`/`conda`), it might be better to just not check them in directly, or if so make the checked-in files specifically for comparison to a per-platform CI check that determines if the output matches the expected (but that latter part would be *optional* for this PR). This could also help automate and decouple the testing from the details of the implementation, which you brought up some valid concerns about—but again, that could go in a future PR.

* [ ] Instead of making all the exception messages base `PythonQtError`s (such that there's no way for tools to programmatically determine whether the module was not installed, not supported on that binding, or some other error occurred) and writing manual messages for each one (which is way more work to add, check and change, and easier to make a typo on), we should subclass it with specific errors of each type, that take a `name` and a `binding` argument (with a `msg` override) and construct the message from there. This also allows us to have it inherit the built-in `ModuleNotFoundError` so it behaves as expected.
  
    Therefore, I suggest adding the following to the `__init__.py` under the existing errors and using them accordingly:

    ```python
     class QtModuleNotFoundError(ModuleNotFoundError, PythonQtError):
        """Raised when a Python Qt binding submodule is not installed/supported."""
        _msg = 'The {name} module was not found for {binding}.'
        _msg_extra = ''

        def __init__(self, *, name, binding=None, msg=None, **msg_kwargs):
            self.binding = binding
            msg = msg or f'{self._msg} {self._msg_extra}'.strip()
            msg = msg.format(name=name, binding=binding, **msg_kwargs)
            super().__init__(msg, name=name)

    class QtBindingMissingModuleError(QtModuleNotFoundError):
        """Raised when a module is not supported by a given binding."""
        _msg_extra = 'It is not currently implemented in {binding}.'

    class QtModuleNotInstalledError(QtModuleNotFoundError):
        """Raise when a module is supported by the binding, but not installed."""
        _msg_extra = 'It must be installed separately'

        def __init__(self, *, missing_package=None, **superclass_kwargs):
            self.missing_package = missing_package
            if missing_package is not None:
                 self._msg_extra += ' as {missing_package}.'
            super().__init__(missing_package=missing_package, **superclass_kwargs)
    ```

Also, a few smaller but common issues (which I made suggestions to fix, except where noted):

* [ ] Let's please not use `*` imports in `module_report`, as they are considered a very bad practice and make the code hard to understand, introspect and lint, among other reasons. It is true that QtPy uses them internally, but for a very specific purpose—ensuring it picks up the full namespace of the packages it wraps without hardcoding—that isn't the case here. Also, we should use standard, conventional PEP 8 import ordering.
* [ ] Module-level constants are ALL_CAPS per PEP 8 and standard convention
* [ ] Don't forget to add terminating EOLs to the files
* [ ] Per PEP 257, docstrings must end with periods and one-line docstrings should be on one line (as they are currently), not with `'''"` above and below (I didn't make review comments for this to avoid noise)

Thanks, and looking forward to seeing this in final form!

At the end of this function we do:

```
    # Return the smaller of the actual thread count and the cgroup's limit, minimum 1.
    result = cast(int, max(1, min(min(affinity_size, cgroup_size), total_machine_size)))
```

So I think if we let `total_machine_size` fall back to 1, we won't ever be able to get an answer above 1, even of e.g. `os.sched_getaffinity()` is available and working properly.

If we really want to properly support cases where `psutil.cpu_count(logical=True)` is unavailable, we need to also change the way we combine the values so that we don't use the CPU count as an upper limit when it's not available.

I haven't looked much at the code in particular but  I did notice that there is only one root `.gitignore` file in the eyes of `get_sources`. This is a problem since files outside of the scope (or should I say folder) of a `.gitignore` file would still be subject to its exclude rules.

Let's say we have a directory structure like this: 
```
.
├── a
│   ├── a.py
│   ├── b.py
│   └── .gitignore
├── a.py
├── b.py
├── c.py
└── .gitignore
```

And with `./.gitignore` containing `c.py` and `./a/.gitignore` containing `b.py`. 

I would expect that only `./c.py` and `./a/b.py` would be ignored by Git and Black. This is true with Git, but not with Black. Black also ignores the root level `b.py` file because the nested `.gitignore`'s contents was added to the "root" `.gitignore` that all the files and directories Black finds recursively are subject to.

```console
(black) ichard26@acer-ubuntu:~/programming/oss/black$ gh pr checkout 1750
(black) ichard26@acer-ubuntu:~/programming/testing/gitignore$ git status
On branch master
Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
	a.py
	a/a.py
	b.py

nothing added to commit but untracked files present (use "git add" to track)
(black) ichard26@acer-ubuntu:~/programming/testing/gitignore$ black .  --check -v
.git ignored: matches the --exclude regular expression
c.py ignored: matches the .gitignore file content
b.py ignored: matches the .gitignore file content
a/b.py ignored: matches the .gitignore file content
/home/ichard26/programming/testing/gitignore/a/a.py already well formatted, good job.
/home/ichard26/programming/testing/gitignore/a.py already well formatted, good job.
All done! ✨ 🍰 ✨
2 files would be left unchanged.
```
---

Perhaps make `gen_python_files` only search through one directory and then return the non-excluded files and subdirectories it found. So similar to `os.walk`. And then on every call to `gen_python_files` in `get_sources` a "`.gitignore` file" is created that makes sense for the current directory it's searching. To make this work you would also have to keep track of the all different `.gitignore` files and their locations.

*I'm sorry if that didn't make any sense at all, I'm not great at explain ideas clearly... Anyway, it's just a suggestion to how you could fix this issue. You probably can think of something better :D

Looking good. I left a few comments and questions.

Regarding `_util` I'm not sure what the problem is. As you say, maybe it is some limitation with object libraries. Since you are building everything into one `libfreud` target, you could instead make the child CMakeList.txt files set a list of source files in a variable and then have the `add_library` compile all those source files together. That would work around any limitations with `OBJECT` and wouldn't result in any files being compiled more than once.

Regarding FindTBB, feel free to use the solution from HOOMD to support finding modern TBB's by their embedded config file (the `find_package_config_first` macro) - that way you don't need to rely on your own `FindTBB.cmake` except on systems like Ubuntu which doesn't provide the TBB config or on platforms with older TBB installations. > Also, remote build doesn't seem to be launching the build when I tested the instructions, not sure what's going on there.

I think what's going on here is this command is really slow if you don't have a clean clone of our repo. `snapcraft` sends everything under your CWD to Launchpad and we often have things like multiple virtual environments (each of which is a ~200MB), multiple clones of the repo under `releases/` or `tests/letstest/`, etc.

You can see this by running `snapcraft remote-build` directly with a command like `snapcraft remote-build --build-on=amd64` from the root of our repo to build the Certbot snap. On a clean clone on my laptop, in about a minute it prints:
```
Packaging sources for certbot...
Packaging sources for shared-metadata...
Sending build data to Launchpad... (https://bradmwarren:<token>@git.launchpad.net/~bradmwarren/+git/snapcraft-certbot-06e0c4/)
Enumerating objects: 12, done.
Counting objects: 100% (12/12), done.
Delta compression using up to 4 threads
Compressing objects: 100% (10/10), done.
Writing objects: 100% (12/12), 59.22 MiB | 938.00 KiB/s, done.
Total 12 (delta 1), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 0
To https://git.launchpad.net/~bradmwarren/+git/snapcraft-certbot-06e0c4/
 * [new branch]      HEAD -> master
If interrupted, resume with: 'snapcraft remote-build --recover'
Building snap package for amd64. This may take some time to finish.
```
In the clone of the Certbot repo I normally use it hangs on the earlier steps much longer.

* Add `helper.tensor_field_for_data_type(TensorProto, DataType)`, `helper.tensor_field_for_numpy_dtype(TensorProto, np.dtype)`. These do all of the mapping lookups and the `getattr` call.
* Move `mapping.py` to `_mapping.py`
* Add a new `mapping.py` that does something like:

```python
import warnings
warnings.warn("onnx.mapping is deprecated and will be removed in a future release. Use functions in onnx.helper instead.", ...)

# for temporary backwards compatibility
from _mapping import *
```

If there's something else in mapping.py that is truly needed by users, we can expose that in `helper.py` as well.

Thank you for your contribution to the project's documentation, it's greatly appreciated. However, before I can accept your pull request, I'd like to ask you to make a few changes:

1. Unfortunately, tables in markdown can often be a suboptimal way to present data. They can be difficult to read and edit, especially in an IDE. It's important that the table structure is as clear and easy to work with as possible. In particular, this means that all columns should be aligned. Please align the column widths based on the widest column to make it easier to read and edit the table for both users in a browser and developers in an IDE.
2. As for the relative links, since the content of this file will be rendered to HTML and displayed separately from its source text, relative links may or may not work properly. In any case, there are currently no relative links in the README.md file, and I would prefer not to introduce additional entropy. Please consider updating the links to absolute URLs where possible, to ensure consistent behavior.

Once again, thank you for your contribution, and I'll be glad to accept your pull request after these suggested changes have been made!

Thanks for taking the time to raise this. However I'm afraid we can't accept this contribution ase already have an API for customizing Pods which you can find [documented here](https://kubernetes.dask.org/en/latest/operator_kubecluster.html#customising-your-cluster).

The high-level API allows you to generate the cluster spec and create the cluster in two separate steps. This allows you to tweak the spec however you like while still benefiting from the high-level API.

For example if you wanted to add a `nodeSelector` you would do it like this.

```python
from dask_kubernetes.operator import KubeCluster, make_cluster_spec

spec = make_cluster_spec(name="selector-example", n_workers=2)
spec["spec"]["worker"]["spec"]["nodeSelector"] = {"disktype": "ssd"}

cluster = KubeCluster(custom_cluster_spec=spec)
```

Nice, 👍 really useful debug output - this will be super helpful in diagnosing user issues more efficiently! 🎉  

The only point we should discuss is whether we want to enable this endpoint by default, or have the user explicitly enable it (e.g., `ENABLE_DIAGNOSTICS`, similar to what we're doing with `ENABLE_CONFIG_UPDATES`). We do strongly recommend our users to always only bind the edge port to 127.0.0.1, but that may not always be the case, unfortunately.

I guess the main issue is that we'd want to enable the diagnostics for an already running instance - because it may be hard to re-create a faulty state, and we don't want to make it a requirement to re-start the container (to enable the environment config). One option would be to allow the user to `docker exec localstack_main touch /tmp/.enable-diagnostics`, which could then override the `ENABLE_DIAGNOSTICS` environment variable.

Don't want to over-engineer the solution here, but security could definitely become a concern here, and we should make a conscious decision.
